d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: King Huan of Zhou interferes in Zheng's internal affairs; Duke Li of Zheng restored
v.s: In 700 BCE, Zheng's minister Zhai Zu seized power and expelled Duke Li of Zheng. King Huan of Zhou attempted to restore royal authority by leading a coalition of feudal armies to interfere in Zheng's internal affairs and restore Duke Li to the throne. However, the Zhou army was defeated by Zheng's general Zhu Dan at the Battle of Xuge, and King Huan himself was wounded by an arrow. Thereafter, the Zhou king lost all prestige, and vassal states ceased to pay court.

v.t: Greek colonists found Acragas (on Sicily)
v.s: Greeks from Gela and Rhodes together founded the city of Acragas on the southern coast of Sicily. It became one of the wealthiest Greek colonies on the island, promoting the expansion of Mediterranean trade networks.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: Assyrian king Sennacherib suppresses Babylonian rebellion
v.s: Babylonian nobles allied with Elamites to rebel against Assyria. Sennacherib marched south and in 689 BCE destroyed Babylon, massacred its inhabitants, and diverted the Euphrates to flood the ruins. This date is about 9 years earlier than the actual event. This extreme action caused strong discontent among Assyrian religious circles and is considered one of the reasons for his later assassination.

d.u: King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin

v.t: King Huan of Zhou dies; his son King Zhuang of Zhou, Ji Tuo, ascends the throne
v.s: In 697 BCE, King Huan of Zhou, Ji Lin, died. During his 23-year reign, he repeatedly fought with Zheng, and the authority of the Son of Heaven further declined. His son Ji Tuo succeeded him as King Zhuang of Zhou. Although King Zhuang tried to restore royal power, the vassal states had already become independent, and the land and troops under the royal house's actual control were very limited.

d.u: King Zhuang of Zhou, Ji Tuo

v.t: King Zhuang of Zhou newly enthroned; civil strife in Wei: princes Xie and Zhi attack and kill Duke Hui of Wei
v.s: In 696 BCE, Duke Hui of Wei had usurped the throne earlier, causing widespread discontent among the clan. With King Zhuang of Zhou newly established, Prince Xie and Prince Zhi allied with the people of Wei to launch a coup and kill Duke Hui. Duke Hui fled to Qi. Wei then fell into years of succession wars.

v.t: Early Iron Age begins in Central Europe's Hallstatt culture
v.s: In Central Europe, the Hallstatt civilization began to widely use iron tools, replacing bronze. Iron smelting technology spread from the Mediterranean, boosting agricultural productivity and weapons innovation.

d.u: King Zhuang of Zhou, Ji Tuo

v.t: Egyptian Pharaoh Psamtik I unifies the Nile Delta
v.s: Psamtik I (r. 664-610 BCE), founder of the 26th Dynasty (Saite Dynasty), used Greek mercenaries to defeat local Delta princes supported by Assyria and completed the unification of Upper and Lower Egypt around 656 BCE, making Sais his capital. This date is about 40 years earlier than the actual unification. Egypt entered a period of revival under Psamtik, known as the 'Saite Renaissance'.

d.u: King Zhuang of Zhou, Ji Tuo

v.t: Duke Huan of Lu meets Duke Xiang of Qi at Luo; Duke Huan of Lu is murdered by Duke Xiang of Qi
v.s: Duke Huan of Lu brought his wife Wen Jiang (sister of Duke Xiang of Qi) to Qi. Duke Xiang of Qi had an affair with Wen Jiang and ordered the strongman Gongzi Pengsheng to pull Duke Huan to death in his chariot. Lu dared not seek revenge but only demanded Pengsheng's life; Duke Xiang killed Pengsheng to cover up the matter.

d.u: King Zhuang of Zhou, Ji Tuo

v.t: Assyrian king Sennacherib orders the rebuilding of Babylon
v.s: A few years after completely destroying Babylon, Sennacherib decided to rebuild the Esagila temple and the city, and grant Babylon a degree of autonomy to pacify the people and gods of Babylonia.

d.u: King Zhuang of Zhou, Ji Tuo

v.t: Elamite king Shutruk-Nakhunte II invades northern Babylon
v.s: Taking advantage of the Assyrian main force fighting in the west, the Elamites raided northern Babylonian cities and plundered large amounts of goods. The Assyrian garrison was unable to counter effectively, causing continued instability in Mesopotamia.

d.u: King Zhuang of Zhou, Ji Tuo

v.t: Assyrian king Sennacherib campaigns against Arabian desert tribes
v.s: Arabian tribes had long harassed Assyrian trade routes. Sennacherib led a camel cavalry deep into the desert, defeated the Qedarites and others, seized water sources, and forced the Arab chieftains to pay tribute.

d.u: King Zhuang of Zhou, Ji Tuo

v.t: Assyrian king Sennacherib moves capital to Nineveh and expands the 'Palace Without Rival'
v.s: To avoid the impact of Babylonian rebellions, Sennacherib formally moved the capital from Kalakh to Nineveh, launched large-scale construction of palaces, and dug canals to bring water into the city. Nineveh became one of the largest cities in the world at that time.

d.u: King Zhuang of Zhou, Ji Tuo

v.t: Corinthian tyrant Cypselus overthrows the Bacchiadae aristocracy and establishes tyranny
v.s: The Bacchiadae family had long monopolized power in Corinth. Cypselus, with the support of commoners and merchants, launched a coup around 657 BCE, overthrowing the aristocracy and becoming the first tyrant of Corinth. This date of -688 is about 31 years earlier than the actual event. He greatly developed trade and colonization, making Corinth one of the most prosperous Greek city-states. His son Periander continued Corinth's golden age.

d.u: King Zhuang of Zhou, Ji Tuo

v.t: Civil strife in Qi: Minister Bao Shuya escorts Prince Xiaobai to Ju; Guan Zhong escorts Prince Jiu to Lu
v.s: Duke Xiang of Qi was tyrannical; his younger brothers feared disaster. Prince Xiaobai, under Bao Shuya's protection, fled to Ju; Prince Jiu, escorted by Guan Zhong and Zhao Hu, went to Lu. Soon, Gongsun Wuzhi assassinated Duke Xiang and declared himself ruler, starting the chaos in Qi.

d.u: King Zhuang of Zhou, Ji Tuo

v.t: Duke Huan of Qi (Xiaobai) ascends the throne and appoints Guan Zhong as prime minister, launching reforms
v.s: Prince Xiaobai returned to Qi before Prince Jiu and was enthroned as Duke Huan. Forgiving the arrow wound, he followed Bao Shuya's advice, redeemed Guan Zhong from Lu, and appointed him prime minister. Guan Zhong implemented military-civil integration, state salt and iron monopolies, and other reforms, laying the foundation for Qi's hegemony.

d.u: King Zhuang of Zhou, Ji Tuo

v.t: Battle of Changshao between Qi and Lu: Cao Gui of Lu discusses strategy and defeats Qi army
v.s: Duke Huan of Qi wanted to avenge the matter of Prince Jiu and attacked Lu. Duke Zhuang of Lu employed Cao Gui, who waited until the Qi army's third drumming showed their morale had waned, then struck. 'One drumbeat, morale raised high' defeated Qi in a classic weak-over-strong battle.

d.u: King Zhuang of Zhou, Ji Tuo

v.t: Athens abolishes lifelong archonship and establishes ten-year archons
v.s: In 682 BCE, tensions between Athenian nobles and commoners intensified. To limit the power of the archons and prevent personal rule, Athens abolished the lifelong archonship and replaced it with a ten-year term. Archons were still elected by the aristocracy, and commoners had no right to vote. This reform was an important step in Athens' transition to democracy.

d.u: King Xi of Zhou, Ji Huqi

v.t: Assyrian king Sennacherib is murdered by his sons; Esarhaddon ascends
v.s: Sennacherib was assassinated by his son Arda-Mulissu while praying in a temple in Nineveh. Esarhaddon suppressed the rebels and succeeded to the throne, immediately rebuilding Babylon (which his father had destroyed) to win popular support.

v.t: Duke Huan of Qi convenes the first interstate meeting at Beixing with Song, Chen, Cai, and Zhu
v.s: In 681 BCE, to settle the rebellion in Song (where Nan Gongwan had killed the ruler), Duke Huan of Qi called a meeting at Beixing (in Qi, present-day Dong'e, Shandong) with the rulers of Song, Chen, Cai, and Zhu. This was Duke Huan's first meeting as a leader of the vassals. Although attendance was small, it marked the beginning of Duke Huan's hegemony.

d.u: King Xi of Zhou, Ji Huqi

v.t: Esarhaddon orders full reconstruction of Babylon and releases deported inhabitants
v.s: To fulfill his promise, Esarhaddon allocated Assyrian treasury funds to repair Babylon's temples and walls and allowed exiled Babylonians to return. Babylon once again submitted to Assyria.

d.u: King Xi of Zhou, Ji Huqi

v.t: Duke Huan of Qi helps rebuild Wei; feudal lords formally recognize Qi as hegemon
v.s: The Di tribes destroyed Wei; only 700 Wei refugees survived. Duke Huan of Qi led allied forces to expel the Di, rebuilt the city at Chuqiu, and installed Duke Dai of Wei. The feudal lords thus honored Duke Huan of Qi as the overlord (Bo).

d.u: King Xi of Zhou, Ji Huqi

v.t: Quwo Wugong attacks and kills Marquis Jin of Jin, unifies Jin; King Xi of Zhou appoints him ruler of Jin
v.s: After three generations of effort, Quwo Wugong killed Marquis Jin and bribed the Zhou king with captured treasures. King Xi appointed Wugong as ruler of Jin, ending the long-standing internal division of Jin.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Assyrian king Esarhaddon campaigns against Phoenicia, captures Sidon
v.s: The Phoenician city-state of Sidon allied with Egypt to resist Assyria. Esarhaddon besieged Sidon for three years with navy and army, then sacked the city and rebuilt a new Sidon as an Assyrian port.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: King Hui of Zhou restored; Zheng and Guo put down Prince Tui's rebellion
v.s: Prince Tui, allied with Wei and Yan, seized the royal city and falsely claimed the throne. Duke Li of Zheng and Duke of Guo united to restore King Hui of Zhou, but the king's authority was again severely damaged.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: King Wen of Chu destroys Xi and captures Xi Gui (Lady Xi)
v.s: In 675 BCE, King Wen of Chu used the excuse that the Marquis of Xi had shown disrespect to Chu to attack and destroy Xi (in present-day Xi County, Henan). Lady Xi Gui (Lady Xi), famous for her beauty, was taken into the Chu king's harem. Although she bore two sons, she remained silent. When asked why, she replied, 'A woman serving two husbands—even if she does not die, what is there to say?'

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Roman king Servius Tullius implements military and political reforms
v.s: Servius Tullius divided citizens into five classes based on property, determining the number of soldiers each class had to provide. He also established the Centuriate Assembly, breaking the aristocracy's monopoly on military power and laying the foundation for the Roman Republic.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Assyrian king Esarhaddon appoints his eldest son Ashurbanipal as crown prince and second son Shamash-shum-ukin as king of Babylon
v.s: To prevent rivalry among his sons, Esarhaddon decreed that Ashurbanipal would inherit Assyria proper and Shamash would rule Babylon. However, the brothers later turned against each other, leading to a major rebellion in Babylon.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Esarhaddon conquers Egypt; Memphis falls
v.s: The Assyrian army crossed the Sinai Peninsula, defeated the Egyptian pharaoh Taharqa at the Battle of Ishqupu, and occupied Memphis. Taharqa fled south, and Assyria directly ruled Egypt for the first time.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Greek colonists establish Cyrene in North Africa
v.s: Famine on Thera (Santorini) led its inhabitants, following a Delphic oracle, under Battus to found Cyrene on the Libyan coast, which later became the most important Greek colony in North Africa.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Assyrian king Esarhaddon dies; Ashurbanipal ascends
v.s: In 669 BCE, King Esarhaddon of the Assyrian Empire died on his way back from Egypt to Nineveh. His son Ashurbanipal succeeded him. Ashurbanipal was the last great king of Assyria. He established the famous library at Nineveh, which collected thousands of cuneiform tablets. He immediately faced a rebellion by his brother Shamash-shum-ukin in Babylon.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Ashurbanipal formally crowned; expels Egyptian forces
v.s: In 668 BCE, Ashurbanipal was formally crowned in Nineveh. He then sent troops to completely expel Pharaoh Taharqa from Lower Egypt and installed pro-Assyrian puppet rulers in Memphis and Thebes. The Assyrian Empire reached its greatest territorial extent at this time.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: King Hui of Zhou grants Duke Huan of Qi the title 'Hou Bo' (overlord), formally recognizing his hegemony
v.s: In 667 BCE, Duke Huan of Qi held an interstate meeting at You, helped the Zhou royal house pacify internal unrest in Fan and other states, and led allied forces against the Rong and Di. To reward his merits, King Hui of Zhou granted him the title 'Hou Bo' (chief of the vassals) and the authority to punish disloyal states on behalf of the Son of Heaven. Duke Huan of Qi became the first recognized hegemon of the Spring and Autumn period.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Chu's prime minister Zi Yuan attacks Zheng; Duke Huan of Qi leads allied forces to relieve Zheng
v.s: In 666 BCE, Chu's prime minister Zi Yuan led 600 chariots north to attack Zheng and entered the outer gate of Zheng. Zheng asked for help from Qi, the hegemon. Duke Huan of Qi led a coalition of Qi, Song, Lu, Zhu, and others to relieve Zheng. When the Chu army heard that the Qi forces were approaching, they withdrew overnight. Zheng was saved. This was the first successful check by Qi of Chu's northward expansion in the Qi-Chu rivalry.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Ashurbanipal campaigns against Arabia, destroys oasis cities
v.s: In 665 BCE, Ashurbanipal led an expedition into northern Arabia. Arabian nomadic tribes had cut off Assyrian trade routes and refused to submit. The Assyrian army penetrated the desert, captured the oasis cities of Tayma and Dedan, and plundered vast numbers of camels and gold and silver. After the campaign, the Arabian tribes were forced to resubmit to Assyria.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Egyptian pharaoh Taharqa dies; Tantamani ascends; Assyria again invades Egypt and sacks Thebes
v.s: In 664 BCE, Kushite pharaoh Taharqa (25th Dynasty) died, and his nephew Tantamani succeeded. Tantamani tried to recapture Lower Egypt from Assyria. Ashurbanipal sent a large army south, which captured and sacked Thebes, looting vast amounts of gold. The 25th Dynasty ended.

v.t: Duke Huan of Qi campaigns north against the Shanrong, saving Yan
v.s: The Shanrong attacked Yan; Duke Zhuang of Yan sought help from Qi. Duke Huan of Qi led an army north, reaching as far as Guzhu (present-day Lulong, Hebei), defeated the Shanrong, and donated the captured land to Yan, ensuring Yan's survival.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Egypt's 26th Dynasty: Psamtik I unifies Egypt, establishes Saite Dynasty
v.s: Psamtik I (r. 664-610 BCE), founder of the 26th Dynasty (Saite Dynasty), used Greek mercenaries to defeat Assyrian-backed local princes in the Delta and completed the unification of Upper and Lower Egypt around 656 BCE, making Sais his capital. This date is about 40 years earlier than the actual unification. Egypt entered a period of revival under Psamtik, known as the 'Saite Renaissance'.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Megarian colonists found Byzantium
v.s: In 660 BCE, Greek colonists from Megara, led by Byzas, founded Byzantium on the European side of the Bosporus Strait. Its location was extremely strategic, controlling the passage between the Black Sea and the Mediterranean. Byzantium later developed into a major commercial center and became Constantinople, the new capital of the Roman Empire, in 330 CE.

v.t: Di destroy Wei; Duke Huan of Qi rescues Wei and moves its capital to Chuqiu
v.s: The Di broke through the Wei capital of Zhao Ge and killed Duke Yi of Wei. Duke Huan of Qi sent his son Wukui with 300 chariots to help the people of Wei recapture their territory and rebuild at Chuqiu, ensuring Wei's survival.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Duke Cheng of Qin dies; his brother Duke Mu of Qin (Renhao) ascends
v.s: In 659 BCE, Duke Cheng of Qin died. Having no sons, his younger brother Renhao succeeded him as Duke Mu of Qin. Duke Mu reigned from 659 to 621 BCE. He employed worthy ministers such as Baili Xi, Jian Shu, and You Yu, expanded west, conquered the Rong tribes, and became the hegemon of the western regions, laying a solid foundation for Qin's eventual unification of China.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Duke Xian of Jin borrows passage from Yu to attack Guo, capturing Xiayang
v.s: In 658 BCE, Duke Xian of Jin, following the advice of his minister Xun Xi, bribed the ruler of Yu with a jade disk and fine horses to request passage through Yu to attack Guo. The greedy ruler of Yu agreed. The Jin army passed through Yu and captured the strategic Guo city of Xiayang (present-day Pinglu, Shanxi). Xun Xi's plan laid the groundwork for later conquests of Guo and Yu.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Duke Huan of Qi convenes vassals at Yanggu to plan an expedition against Chu
v.s: In 657 BCE, Chu's northward expansion threatened Zheng and Xu. Duke Huan of Qi called a meeting of the rulers of Lu, Song, Chen, Wei, Zheng, Xu, Cao, and others at Yanggu (present-day Yanggu, Shandong) to discuss a coordinated military response to Chu. This Yanggu meeting was diplomatic preparation for attacking Chu. The following year, Duke Huan led an eight-state coalition against Chu, forcing Chu to sign a treaty at Shaoling.

v.t: Cypselus dies; his son Periander succeeds as tyrant of Corinth
v.s: In 657 BCE, Cypselus, tyrant of Corinth, died and was succeeded by his son Periander. Periander ruled Corinth for forty years (c. 627–587 BCE) and was one of the 'Seven Sages of Greece'. He greatly developed trade and colonization, built a portage road (diolkos) across the Isthmus, and made Corinth one of the richest and most powerful Greek city-states. However, his rule was also quite brutal.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Duke Huan of Qi leads eight-state coalition against Chu; Chu envoy Qu Wan concludes alliance at Shaoling
v.s: The Qi-led coalition advanced to Mount Xing. King Cheng of Chu sent Qu Wan to negotiate at Shaoling. Qu Wan said, 'If you, my lord, rule the vassals by virtue, who would dare not submit?' Duke Huan, seeing that the Chu army was in good order, made peace with Chu, and Chu recognized Qi's hegemony.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Duke Xian of Jin destroys Guo through Yu's territory and on the return march destroys Yu; Xun Xi's plan succeeds
v.s: In 655 BCE, Duke Xian of Jin again followed Xun Xi's plan, using famous horses and jade disks to bribe Yu for passage to attack Guo. After destroying Guo, the Jin army, on its return, launched a surprise attack on unsuspecting Yu, capturing the Duke of Yu and his minister Baili Xi. Xun Xi retrieved the treasures that had been given to Yu, achieving the classic stratagem of 'lend a road to destroy Guo, and destroy Yu as well, like lips and teeth'.

v.t: Ashurbanipal establishes a great library at Nineveh, collecting cuneiform tablets
v.s: Ashurbanipal sent scholars to Babylonia to copy ancient texts, collecting tens of thousands of clay tablets containing epics, science, and religion, preserving much of Mesopotamian knowledge for posterity.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Greek colonists establish colonies on the Hellespont, controlling the Black Sea route
v.s: In 654 BCE, Greek colonists from Miletus and other cities founded Abydos and other colonies on the north shore of the Hellespont (the Dardanelles). These colonies controlled the strait from the Aegean to the Black Sea, allowing Greek merchants to trade with nomadic peoples on the Black Sea coast for grain, timber, and slaves. The Black Sea route became a vital economic lifeline for the Greek world.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Elamite king Te-hum-um-Igigi is captured by Assyria; Elam becomes a vassal
v.s: In 653 BCE, Assyrian king Ashurbanipal invaded Elam (present-day southwestern Iran) and defeated the Elamite army at the Battle of Dur-Untash, capturing King Te-hum-um-Igigi. The prisoners and great booty were taken back to Nineveh. Elam thereafter became a vassal state of the Assyrian Empire, losing its independence.

d.u: King Hui of Zhou, Ji Lang

v.t: Babylonian king Shamash-shum-ukin allies with Elam and Chaldea to rebel against Assyria
v.s: In 652 BCE, Ashurbanipal's brother, the Babylonian king Shamash-shum-ukin, allied with Elam, the Chaldeans, Arab tribes, and parts of Syria to launch a major rebellion against Assyria. The rebels controlled most of Babylonia and cut communications between the two rivers. Ashurbanipal mobilized his main army to counterattack and besieged Babylon for two years.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Duke Huan of Qi convenes the lords of Lu, Song, Wei, Zheng, Xu, and Cao at Kuiqiu; his hegemony reaches its peak
v.s: King Xiang of Zhou sent Zai Kong to present sacrificial meat to Duke Huan. Duke Huan descended the steps to bow, but did not need to kneel. The covenant had five articles: punish unfilial acts, honor the worthy and educate the talented, do not obstruct valleys, do not change the legitimate heir, and do not make a concubine a wife. Duke Huan of Qi was formally recognized as the overlord of the realm.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Phoenician sailors, under Pharaoh Necho II, complete the first circumnavigation of Africa
v.s: Necho II organized a Phoenician fleet that sailed from the Red Sea down the east coast of Africa, rounded the Cape of Good Hope, and returned via the Straits of Gibraltar to Egypt, taking three years, proving that Libya (Africa) is surrounded by sea.

v.t: Iron smelting technology rapidly spreads across the Eurasian steppes; Scythians dramatically increase their iron weapons
v.s: Around 650 BCE, iron smelting technology spread from the Caucasus and Central Asia to the nomadic tribes of the Eurasian steppes. The Scythians mastered forging techniques and mass-produced iron arrowheads, horse gear, short swords (akinakes), and scale armor. Iron weapons were harder, sharper, and easier to repair than bronze, greatly enhancing the combat effectiveness of Scythian cavalry, allowing them to dominate the vast steppe region from the Black Sea to the Altai.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Duke Mu of Qin supports Prince Yiwu's return to Jin to ascend the throne
v.s: After Duke Xian of Jin died and the Li Ji turmoil, Yiwu sought Qin's help to return, promising to cede the Hexi region. Duke Mu of Qin sent troops to escort Yiwu back to Jin, where he became Duke Hui of Jin. However, Duke Hui reneged on his promise and did not cede the land.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Ashurbanipal captures Babylon; his brother Shamash-shum-ukin commits suicide by fire
v.s: After a long siege, the Assyrian army broke into Babylon. The rebel king Shamash-shum-ukin set himself on fire in his palace. Ashurbanipal sacked the city in revenge, and Babylon lost all independence.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Ashurbanipal destroys Susa; Elam is completely extinguished
v.s: In 647 BCE, to punish Elam for supporting the Babylonian rebels, Ashurbanipal led an army deep into Elam and captured its capital Susa. The Assyrians destroyed the palaces and temples of Susa, dug up the tombs of Elamite kings, and carried away Elamite statues to Assyria. The complete destruction of Susa marked the end of Elam as an independent state. Ashurbanipal boasted in his inscriptions: 'The whole of Elam I have laid waste.'

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Median king Phraortes attacks northern Assyria, is defeated and killed
v.s: The Median kingdom began to expand. Phraortes led an army against Assyrian Nineveh but was routed by the Assyrians and killed. His son Cyaxares succeeded him.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Li Ji turmoil in Jin: Crown Prince Shensheng commits suicide; Chong'er and Yiwu flee
v.s: Duke Xian of Jin's favored concubine Li Ji wanted her son Xiqi to be heir. She falsely accused Crown Prince Shensheng of poisoning Duke Xian. Shensheng hanged himself at Xincheng. Chong'er fled to Pu, and Yiwu fled to Qu, sparking civil war in Jin.

v.t: The Library of Nineveh is completed, becoming the largest repository of knowledge in the ancient world
v.s: Ashurbanipal ordered over 20,000 clay tablets collected from various places to be stored in the Nineveh palace, classified by subject, including grammar, medicine, astronomy, and the Epic of Gilgamesh.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: King Cheng of Chu attacks Xu; Duke Huan of Qi leads allied forces to relieve Xu
v.s: In 644 BCE, King Cheng of Chu, seeking to expand north, attacked Xu (present-day Si County, Anhui). Xu was an eastern member of the Central Plains alliance. Hegemon Duke Huan of Qi consequently led a coalition of Lu, Song, Chen, Wei, Zheng, Xu, Cao, and others to rescue Xu. The allied forces confronted the Chu army on Xu's border for months, and the Chu army eventually withdrew. This successful rescue maintained unity among the Central Plains alliance.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Duke Huan of Qi dies; five sons contend for the throne, causing great chaos in Qi
v.s: When Duke Huan was critically ill, Yi Ya and Diao Diao sealed the palace gates. Duke Huan starved to death. His body was not buried until 67 days later when worms crawled out of the door. His sons Wukui, Zhao, Pan, Yuan, and Shang fought each other, and Duke Xiang of Song intervened.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Duke Xiang of Song quells the Qi chaos and establishes Duke Xiao of Qi
v.s: In 642 BCE, Duke Xiang of Song, styling himself hegemon, led troops from Song, Cao, Wei, and Zhu to escort Duke Huan's legitimate heir, Prince Zhao (Duke Xiao of Qi), back to Qi. Other Qi princes resisted, but the Song army defeated them at the Battle of Yan, establishing Prince Zhao as Duke Xiao. This action raised Duke Xiang's prestige among the Central Plains rulers but also provoked Chu's displeasure.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Assyrian king Ashurbanipal dies; the Assyrian Empire rapidly declines
v.s: In 641 BCE (actually around 631 BCE), Ashurbanipal, the last great king of Assyria, died. He had built a vast empire from Egypt to Iran and built the famous library at Nineveh. However, after his death, the empire quickly declined due to internal divisions, border rebellions, and a combined Median-Babylonian counterattack. Twenty years later (612 BCE), Nineveh fell.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Lydian king Alyattes mints the world's first metal coins
v.s: Around 640 BCE, Alyattes, king of Lydia in Asia Minor, ordered the minting of the world's first coins made of electrum (a gold-silver alloy). These elliptical coins, stamped with a lion's head, weighed about 4.7 grams each. The invention of coinage greatly facilitated commerce, and Lydia's capital Sardis became a commercial center of the ancient world. His son Croesus later minted standard pure gold and silver coins.

v.t: Rome goes to war with the Latin League; Rome gradually takes control of the Latin plain
v.s: To gain control of Alba Longa's legacy, Rome clashed with cities of the Latin League. King Tullius of Rome, after several battles, forced some Latin cities to accept Roman hegemony.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Ashurbanipal conquers Elam, captures Susa, and carries off Elamite statues
v.s: In 639 BCE, Ashurbanipal launched a final, devastating campaign against rebellious Elam. The Assyrian army penetrated deep into Elam, again captured Susa, and plundered the statues of the Elamite goddess Nanaya and others. Ashurbanipal's palace reliefs at Nineveh detailed this campaign. It was Assyria's last major victory; thereafter the empire quickly declined.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Battle of Hongshui between Song and Chu: Duke Xiang of Song, refusing to attack before the enemy is fully arrayed, suffers a crushing defeat and later dies of his wounds
v.s: In 638 BCE, Duke Xiang of Song led his army to confront the Chu army at the Hong River (present-day Zhecheng, Henan). The Song army arrived first and formed ranks on the north bank. The Chu army was crossing the river. The commander, Gongsun Gu, suggested attacking while they were half across, but Duke Xiang refused, citing the principle that a gentleman does not take advantage of the distressed. After the Chu army had fully crossed and was still forming ranks, Gongsun Gu again pleaded to attack, but Duke Xiang again refused. Only when the Chu army had fully formed did Duke Xiang order the attack. The Song army was routed, and the Duke was hit in the leg by an arrow. He died the following year from his wounds.

v.t: Athens establishes a nine-archon council, further weakening royal power
v.s: In 638 BCE, the Athenian aristocracy undertook a political reform, expanding the number of archons from three to nine, each handling specific affairs: the King Archon (religious), the Polemarch (military), the Archon (judicial), and six Thesmothetai (administrative/legal). The king (basileus), who once held supreme power, was left only religious functions. This reform left the Athenian monarchy powerless in name, and an aristocratic republic was essentially established.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Jin prince Chong'er goes into exile in Qi; Duke Huan of Qi treats him generously
v.s: In 637 BCE, Prince Chong'er of Jin (later Duke Wen of Jin), after nineteen years of exile, arrived in Qi. Duke Huan of Qi received him with great ceremony, married a daughter of the clan to him, and presented him with twenty teams of horses (eighty horses). Chong'er lived a comfortable life in Qi and was reluctant to continue his exile. However, his followers Zhao Cui and Hu Yan plotted to get him drunk and carry him away, thus continuing his journey to seek support among the states.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Jin prince Chong'er returns to Jin with the support of Duke Mu of Qin and ascends the throne as Duke Wen of Jin
v.s: In 636 BCE, Prince Chong'er of Jin, after nineteen years of exile, returned to Jin under military escort from Duke Mu of Qin. Duke Mu sent troops to cross the Yellow River and escorted Chong'er to Quwo (present-day Wenxi, Shanxi), where he became Duke Wen of Jin. Chong'er was then 62 years old. After ascending, he employed worthy ministers such as Zhao Cui, Hu Yan, and Xian Zhen to reorganize internal affairs and develop the military. Jin quickly rose as a major power in the Central Plains. Four years later (632 BCE), Duke Wen defeated Chu at the Battle of Chengpu.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Duke Wen of Jin puts down Prince Dai's rebellion and restores King Xiang of Zhou
v.s: Prince Dai, allied with the Di, attacked the royal city. King Xiang of Zhou fled to Zheng. Duke Wen of Jin led an army to the king's aid, killed Prince Dai, and escorted King Xiang back to Luoyi. The king granted Yangfan, Wen, Yuan, and other lands to Jin.

v.t: Greek colonists found Istria on the west coast of the Black Sea
v.s: In 635 BCE, Greek colonists from Miletus founded the city of Istria on the west coast of the Black Sea (in present-day Romania/Bulgaria border region), near the Danube Delta. Istria was one of the earliest Greek colonies on the western Black Sea coast, serving as an important trading post between Greeks and local Getae and Scythians. The city exported Greek pottery, olive oil, and wine, and imported grain, furs, and slaves.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: King Cheng of Chu attacks Song; Song asks for help from Jin; Duke Wen of Jin prepares to rescue Song
v.s: In 634 BCE, King Cheng of Chu, allied with Chen, Cai, Zheng, and Xu, besieged Song's capital. Duke Cheng of Song appealed for help to Duke Wen of Jin, who had just ascended. Duke Wen recalled that when he had been exiled in Chu, the Chu king treated him generously. Nevertheless, he could not allow Song to be destroyed. Following Xian Zhen's advice, he attacked Chu's allies Cao and Wei to relieve Song, leading to the Battle of Chengpu that decided the Jin-Chu struggle.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Prelude to the Battle of Chengpu: Jin attacks Cao and Wei; Chu army is forced north
v.s: Duke Wen of Jin attacked Cao and Wei, giving their lands to Song. The Chu army had besieged several Song cities. Jin brought Qi and Qin into the mediation. King Cheng of Chu did not want to fight Jin and ordered Ziyu to withdraw, but Ziyu disobeyed.

v.t: The Assyrian Empire completely collapses; Nineveh is besieged by the Medes and Neo-Babylonians
v.s: In 633 BCE (actually 612 BCE), Median king Cyaxares and Neo-Babylonian king Nabopolassar united to besiege Nineveh, the Assyrian capital. The last Assyrian king, Sin-shar-ishkun, defended stubbornly. The Assyrian walls were high and strong; the allies used siege engines and water tactics. After three months, the Tigris flooded and broke down part of the wall, allowing the allies to storm the city. The mighty Assyrian Empire was thus destroyed.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Battle of Chengpu: Duke Wen of Jin retreats 90 li (three 'she'), then crushes the Chu army; the Treaty of Jiantu establishes Jin's hegemony
v.s: The allied armies of Jin, Song, Qi, and Qin fought the combined forces of Chu, Chen, and Cai at Chengpu. The Jin army retreated 90 li to avoid the Chu army's initial momentum, then attacked from both flanks, encircling and defeating them. Ziyu committed suicide. Duke Wen of Jin presented Chu prisoners to the Zhou king, who appointed Jin as the overlord. The feudal lords met at Jiantu.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Greek colonists found Cyrene, an important Greek city-state in North Africa
v.s: In 631 BCE, Greek colonists from Thera (Santorini), led by Battus I, founded Cyrene on the Libyan coast. Cyrene quickly became one of the wealthiest Greek city-states in North Africa, renowned for its production of the precious herb silphium (an ancient contraceptive). Cyrene maintained complex trade and diplomatic relations with the local Libyan tribes and later became an important cultural center of the Hellenistic age.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Sparta suppresses the Messenian revolt, reducing the Messenians to helots
v.s: Around 630 BCE, during the Second Messenian War, Sparta crushed the Messenian revolt. The Spartan army captured the last Messenian fortress of Mount Ithome. All Messenians were captured and reduced to the status of state-owned slaves (helots), who worked the land for the Spartans. The helot population greatly outnumbered Spartan citizens, forcing Sparta to establish a unique social system focused on military training and repression.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Jin-Qin relations break down; Qin begins to plan eastward expansion
v.s: In 629 BCE, after the death of Duke Wen of Jin, Duke Mu of Qin decided to expand eastward. His strategy was to cross Jin territory to gain hegemony in the Central Plains. Ignoring the opposition of old ministers Baili Xi and Jian Shu, Duke Mu sent Meng Mingshi to attack Zheng from afar. The new ruler of Jin, Duke Xiang of Jin, laid an ambush at Mount Xiao (present-day Shan County, Henan), completely destroying the Qin army. Relations between Qin and Jin were permanently broken, and the Battle of Xiao marked the beginning of long-term confrontation between the two states.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Duke Wen of Jin dies; his son Huan ascends as Duke Xiang of Jin
v.s: In 628 BCE, Duke Wen of Jin, one of the Five Hegemons of the Spring and Autumn period, died after only nine years of reign (636–628 BCE). His son Huan succeeded him as Duke Xiang of Jin. Duke Wen had become hegemon of the Central Plains by defeating Chu at the Battle of Chengpu and had strengthened Jin through internal reforms. Duke Xiang continued his father's hegemony, destroying the Qin army at the Battle of Xiao and thwarting Qin's eastward ambitions.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Battle of Xiao between Qin and Jin: Jin forces annihilate the Qin army, thwarting Qin's eastward advance
v.s: Duke Mu of Qin ignored Jian Shu's advice and sent three generals to attack Zheng. On the way, they were discovered by the Zheng merchant Xian Gao, who feigned a welcome. On their return, they passed Mount Xiao, where they fell into a Jin ambush and were completely wiped out. The three generals were captured. Qin then turned its attention to expanding westward against the Rong.

v.t: The Assyrian Empire falls; Nineveh is captured by the coalition; the last king is killed
v.s: After two years of siege, the combined forces of the Neo-Babylonians and Medes used a flood to breach the walls of Nineveh. The Assyrian king set himself on fire in his palace. The Assyrian Empire was destroyed, its territory divided.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Nabopolassar establishes the Neo-Babylonian Empire and formally declares himself king
v.s: In 626 BCE, Nabopolassar, the Assyrian governor of Babylon, took advantage of Assyrian civil strife to declare independence in Babylon and proclaim himself king of Babylon. He founded the Chaldean dynasty (Neo-Babylonian Empire) with Babylon as his capital. He then allied with Median king Cyaxares to launch the final attack on the dying Assyrian Empire. His son Nebuchadnezzar II would later bring the empire to its peak.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Median king Cyaxares implements military reforms, creating a combined army of cavalry and infantry
v.s: In 625 BCE, Cyaxares of Media carried out a crucial military reform. He transformed the traditional tribal levies into a regular army organized by branches: spearmen, archers, cavalry, and coordinated tactics. This reform greatly increased the fighting power of the Median army, allowing Media to rapidly emerge as a major military power in Western Asia and providing the military foundation for the destruction of Assyria.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Duke Mu of Qin attacks Jin, burns his boats after crossing the Yellow River to show determination, and captures Wangguan
v.s: To avenge the defeat at Xiao, Duke Mu of Qin led a large army east, crossed the Yellow River, and burned the boats. He captured several cities including Wangguan in Jin. The Jin army held fast behind their walls. Duke Mu then went to Mount Xiao to mourn the fallen soldiers and returned home.

v.t: Athenian aristocrat Draco is appointed lawgiver and issues harsh laws
v.s: The Draconian code prescribed death for all crimes, even stealing a cabbage, giving rise to the term 'Draconian'. However, this was the first written code, limiting the arbitrary interpretation of law by the nobility.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Duke Mu of Qin conquers twelve Rong states in the west, becoming hegemon of the west; the Zhou king sends him a golden drum
v.s: In 623 BCE, Duke Mu of Qin, following the advice of You Yu, launched a major campaign against the Western Rong. The Qin army conquered twelve Rong states, including Mianzhu, Di, and Huan, expanding territory by a thousand li, and became the hegemon of the western regions. King Xiang of Zhou sent the official Zhao Guo with a golden drum to congratulate him, formally recognizing Duke Mu's hegemony in the west. This greatly increased Qin's territory and power, laying the foundation for its eventual unification of China.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Ancient Greek philosopher Thales is born in Miletus
v.s: Around 624–620 BCE, Thales, the first philosopher of ancient Greece, was born in the city-state of Miletus in Asia Minor. Thales proposed that water is the fundamental principle (arche) of all things, a materialist proposition. He also accurately predicted the solar eclipse of 585 BCE and introduced Egyptian geometry to Greece. Thales is regarded as the father of Western philosophy and science.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Athens promulgates the Draconian code, the first written law
v.s: In 621 BCE, the Athenian archon Draco presided over the drafting of Athens' first written law code, the Draconian code. This code put customary law into writing, ending the aristocracy's arbitrary interpretation of laws. However, the code was known for its harshness, prescribing death for nearly all offenses (called 'written in blood'). Although later superseded by Solon's reforms, the Draconian code was significant as the beginning of written law.

v.t: Duke Mu of Qin dies with 177 human sacrifices, including three worthy ministers of the Zi-che family
v.s: In 621 BCE, Duke Mu of Qin, one of the Five Hegemons, died. He had reigned for 39 years, expanding west and making Qin the hegemon of the west. At his death, following the backward Qin custom of human sacrifice, 177 people were buried with him, including three worthy ministers – Zi-che Yansi, Zi-che Zhongxing, and Zi-che Zhenhu (the 'Three Good Men'). The people of Qin mourned deeply, and the poem 'Yellow Bird' in the Book of Songs mourns this event. The practice of human sacrifice put Qin at a disadvantage in the competition for talent.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: Zhao Dun of Jin takes power, marking the rise of the Zhao clan
v.s: Duke Xiang of Jin died. Zhao Dun wanted to install Prince Yong but was opposed by Jia Ji. Eventually, the young Yigao was installed as Duke Ling of Jin, with Zhao Dun controlling military and political affairs, beginning the era of ministerial dominance in Jin.

d.u: King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng

v.t: King Xiang of Zhou dies; his son King Qing of Zhou, Ji Renchen, ascends
v.s: In 619 BCE, King Xiang of Zhou, Ji Zheng, died. He had reigned for 32 years, during which Duke Huan of Qi and Duke Wen of Jin successively became hegemons, and the Zhou royal authority further declined. His son Ji Renchen succeeded him as King Qing of Zhou. When King Qing came to power, the royal finances were so straitened that he could not afford a proper funeral for King Xiang and had to beg for financial aid from Lu.

d.u: King Qing of Zhou, Ji Renchen

v.t: King Mu of Chu attacks Zheng; Duke Mu of Zheng makes peace, extending Chu influence deep into the Central Plains
v.s: In 618 BCE, King Mu of Chu, Shang Chen, led a large Chu army north to attack Zheng (present-day Xinzheng, Henan). Zheng was an important Central Plains state, often caught between the two great powers of Jin and Chu. At that time, Duke Ling of Jin was a young child (under ten), so Jin was unable to rescue Zheng. Duke Mu of Zheng was forced to sign a humiliating treaty with Chu, becoming a vassal of Chu. Thus, Chu power penetrated deep into the Central Plains.

d.u: King Qing of Zhou, Ji Renchen

v.t: King Mu of Chu meets with the rulers of Cai, Chen, and Zheng at Jueyi, establishing Chu's hegemony over the Central Plains
v.s: In 617 BCE, King Mu of Chu convened a meeting with the rulers of Cai (Duke Zhuang), Chen (Duke Gong), and Zheng (Duke Mu) at Jueyi (present-day Xiangcheng, Henan). Duke Zhao of Song was forced to greet the Chu king with the special 'Mengzhu deer' ceremony, recognizing Chu's hegemony in the Central Plains. This was the first time Chu presided over a meeting of Central Plains vassals as hegemon. Although Jin was unhappy, it was unable to intervene due to its weak ruler and strong ministers.

d.u: King Qing of Zhou, Ji Renchen

v.t: Etruscan Lucumo seizes power in Rome and becomes king, taking the name Tarquinius Priscus
v.s: The Etruscans, using their wealth and military power, took control of Rome, establishing the Tarquin dynasty. Rome entered a period of Etruscan rule, with major construction projects such as the Cloaca Maxima (Great Sewer).

d.u: King Qing of Zhou, Ji Renchen

v.t: Duke Xiang of Yan moves the capital to Ji City, strengthening control over northern China
v.s: In 615 BCE, under Duke Xiang of Yan, the state of Yan moved its capital from an earlier site to Ji City (southwest of present-day Beijing). Ji City was strategically located at the northern edge of the North China Plain and the eastern foot of the Taihang Mountains, ideal for defense against northern Rong and Di and for expansion into the Central Plains. This move allowed Yan to strengthen its control over the northern part of North China, laying the foundation for Yan becoming one of the Seven Warring States.

d.u: King Qing of Zhou, Ji Renchen

v.t: Median army captures the Assyrian ancient city of Arapkha; Assyrian remnants retreat to Harran
v.s: In 614 BCE, Median king Cyaxares led a combined Median-Babylonian army to capture the important Assyrian ancient city of Arapkha (present-day Kirkuk, Iraq). The last Assyrian king, Sin-shar-ishkun, led his remaining forces into upper Mesopotamia, retreating to Harran (present-day southeastern Turkey). All the core territories of Assyria were lost, and the Assyrian state lingered only in Harran.

d.u: King Qing of Zhou, Ji Renchen

v.t: King Zhuang of Chu ascends the throne; after three years of silence, he startles all
v.s: In 613 BCE, King Mu of Chu died, and his son Xiong Lü succeeded him as King Zhuang of Chu. For the first three years, King Zhuang was mired in wine and pleasure, neglecting government. The ministers of Chu were worried. The minister Shen Wuwu used the allegory of a bird that does not fly or sing for three years and asked what kind of bird it was. King Zhuang replied, 'For three years it does not fly; when it flies, it will soar to the sky. For three years it does not sing; when it sings, it will startle all.' He then energetically pursued reforms, marched north, and eventually 'inquired about the weight of the Nine Tripods,' becoming one of the Five Hegemons.

d.u: King Qing of Zhou, Ji Renchen

v.t: Nineveh falls to the Medes and Babylonians; the Assyrian Empire is completely destroyed
v.s: The allies used a flood to breach the city walls of Nineveh. The Assyrian king Sin-shar-ishkun committed suicide. The Assyrian Empire was destroyed, and its territory was divided between the Neo-Babylonians and Medes.

d.u: King Qing of Zhou, Ji Renchen

v.t: Zhao Dun of Jin leads a coalition to attack Zheng because Zheng abandoned Jin for Chu
v.s: In 611 BCE, Zhao Dun, the chief minister of Jin, led a coalition of Jin, Song, Wei, and Chen to attack Zheng. The reason was that Duke Ling of Zheng, under pressure from Chu, had abandoned his alliance with Jin and turned to Chu. The coalition besieged Zheng's capital, and Zheng was forced to sue for peace. Zhao Dun accepted, but soon Zheng again switched sides due to Chu pressure. Zheng's shuttling between the two great powers of Jin and Chu became a major geopolitical feature of the mid-Spring and Autumn period.

d.u: King Kuang of Zhou, Ji Ban

v.t: Egyptian pharaoh Necho II expels Babylonian garrisons and invades Palestine
v.s: In 610 BCE, Egyptian pharaoh Necho II took advantage of the power vacuum left by the collapse of Assyria to lead his army north along the eastern Mediterranean coast, expelling Babylonian garrisons from Palestine. The Egyptian army captured Gaza and Jaffa, restoring Egyptian control over Palestine and the Phoenician coast. Necho II also began preliminary work on a canal from the Nile to the Red Sea.

v.t: Natural philosophy emerges in the Milesian school
v.s: Around 610 BCE, the Milesian school of philosophers emerged in the Greek city-state of Miletus, represented by Thales, Anaximander, and Anaximenes. They attempted to explain the world through natural causes rather than myth, proposing water, the infinite (apeiron), and air as the fundamental principle (arche) of all things. The Milesian school marked a turning point in Western thought from mythological to rational thinking.

d.u: King Kuang of Zhou, Ji Ban

v.t: Egyptian pharaoh Necho II defeats and kills King Josiah of Judah at Megiddo
v.s: In 609 BCE, Egyptian pharaoh Necho II marched north to aid the remnants of the Assyrian army against the Babylonians. King Josiah of Judah, an ally of Babylon, blocked the Egyptian advance at Megiddo (in present-day northern Israel). The Egyptian and Judahite armies clashed on the plain of Megiddo, and Josiah was mortally wounded by an arrow. Thereafter, Judah became a vassal of Egypt.

d.u: King Kuang of Zhou, Ji Ban

v.t: Qi attacks Ju; Ju sues for peace; Qi expands eastward
v.s: In 608 BCE, Duke Qing of Qi, early in his reign, sought to revive the hegemony of Duke Huan of Qi. He attacked the eastern neighbor Ju (present-day Ju County, Shandong). The Qi army captured several border towns of Ju. Under strong military pressure, Ju was forced to sue for peace and become a vassal, ceding some territory. Through this campaign, Qi expanded its sphere of influence in the eastern part of the Shandong Peninsula.

d.u: King Kuang of Zhou, Ji Ban

v.t: Zhao Chuan, cousin of Zhao Dun of Jin, assassinates Duke Ling of Jin; Zhao Dun returns and establishes Duke Cheng; the historian records 'Zhao Dun assassinated his ruler'
v.s: Duke Ling of Jin was cruel and tyrannical. Zhao Dun left the capital to avoid trouble. Before he had crossed the border, Duke Ling was killed. The grand historian Dong Hu wrote in the record: 'Zhao Dun assassinated his ruler.' Zhao Dun had no choice but to establish Prince Heitun as Duke Cheng of Jin.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: King Zhuang of Chu attacks the Lu-hun Rong, stations his army on Zhou territory, and asks about the weight of the Nine Tripods
v.s: King Zhuang of Chu, under the pretext of attacking the Lu-hun Rong, led his army to the Luo River and conducted a military review on the border of the Zhou royal domain. King Ding of Zhou sent Wangsun Man to offer provisions. King Zhuang of Chu asked about the size and weight of the Nine Tripods (symbols of royal authority). Wangsun Man replied, 'Virtue, not the tripods, is what matters.'

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: Babylonian king Nabopolassar dies; Nebuchadnezzar II ascends and crushes Egypt at the Battle of Carchemish
v.s: Nebuchadnezzar II defeated the Egyptian pharaoh Necho II's army at Carchemish on the upper Euphrates, driving Egyptian forces out completely. Syria-Palestine came under Neo-Babylonian control.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: Nebuchadnezzar II formally becomes king of Babylon; begins massive building projects
v.s: In 604 BCE, Nabopolassar, founder of the Neo-Babylonian Empire, died and was succeeded by his son Nebuchadnezzar II. Immediately after ascending, he launched one of the most ambitious building programs in ancient history: construction of the Ishtar Gate, Processional Way, the Hanging Gardens (one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World), Babylon's walls, and huge palace complexes. Under him, Babylon became the most magnificent city of the ancient world.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: King Zhuang of Chu attacks Chen, executes Xia Zhengshu, initially intends to annex Chen but later restores it, winning praise from the feudal lords
v.s: Xia Zhengshu, a minister of Chen, assassinated Duke Ling of Chen. King Zhuang of Chu led an army to punish him, executing Xia Zhengshu by dismemberment. He initially wanted to annex Chen, but following the advice of Shen Shushi, he restored Chen. The feudal lords were impressed and submitted to Chu.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: The Yellow River changes course at Qi County, causing major flooding; vassal states jointly build dikes
v.s: In 602 BCE, the Yellow River changed its course near Qi County (present-day Qi County, Henan), the first clearly documented course change in history. The original course flowed east to Xun County then north into the Bohai Sea. After the change, the main stream flowed east to Hua County then turned northeast. The change caused widespread flooding, submerging farmland and villages. The states of Qi, Lu, Song, and Wei were forced to organize laborers to build new dikes, the first joint effort by Chinese states to control the Yellow River.

d.u: King Ding of Zhou, Ji Yu

v.t: King Zhuang of Chu defeats Jin at the Battle of Bi, establishing Chu's hegemony
v.s: King Zhuang of Chu led an army to attack Zheng; Duke Jing of Jin sent Xun Linfu to rescue Zheng. The Jin army was divided internally. The Chu army launched a surprise attack while the Jin army was crossing the river, routing them. After the Battle of Bi, King Zhuang of Chu became hegemon.

v.t: Greek lyric poet Sappho flourishes on Lesbos, composing love poetry
v.s: Around 600 BCE, the most famous female poet of ancient Greece, Sappho, composed lyric poetry in Mytilene on the island of Lesbos. Sappho ran a school for aristocratic girls teaching poetry and music. Her poetry, especially love poetry, was emotional and refined, leading Plato to call her the 'Tenth Muse'. Sappho was highly esteemed in ancient Greece, creating the Sapphic stanza and profoundly influencing later Western literature.
